title: Why This Needless Hesitation to Bring the Launch Pad Here?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The OCR-derived title `எவுளம்` was verified from the source as `ஏவுதளம்`. Additional source-visible OCR errors affecting place names and ordinary Tamil words, including `ஸ்ரீஹரிகோட்டா`, `இஸ்ரோவைச் சேர்ந்த`, `மண்டலமாக`, `நன்மைகளெல்லாம்`, and `நல்லது`, are restored in the derived Tamil reading copy. The English passage quoted from the parliamentary record is preserved as printed. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: On 19 August 2013, I wrote to Prime Minister Dr Manmohan Singh, recommending a request sent to Rajya Sabha member and poet Kanimozhi by employees of the Liquid Propulsion Centre at Mahendragiri in Tirunelveli district. Kanimozhi not only gave me their petition, but also raised a question about it in the Rajya Sabha. The reply placed on record stated:

> ISRO has two operational launching pads located at Satish Dhawan Space Centre, Sriharikota - the First Launch Pad and the Second Launch Pad, with necessary infra structure to support eight launches per year. The launching infra structure at Sriharikota will be strengthened with the addition of Second Vehicle Assembly Building at SLP, which will enhance the launch frequency to twelve launches per year by 2017

In my letter to the Prime Minister, I requested that an Indian Institute for Space and Liquid-Propulsion Technology be established at Mahendragiri in Tirunelveli district, and that ISRO's second rocket launch pad be established at Kulasekarapattinam in Thoothukudi district.

The Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology established by ISRO in Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, gives importance only to technologies connected with space. In the present circumstances, however, it is both urgent and important to establish an institute giving priority to space science and liquid propulsion.

I have been informed that our country has only a very small number of specialists in space and liquid-propulsion technology. A liquid-propulsion centre at Mahendragiri would be a qualified specialist institution for courses related to aircraft design, helicopter design, the development of high-speed aircraft and space-technology research. I therefore wish to recommend its establishment. I also wish to point out that the required land and the technical expertise needed to advise its implementation are available there.
ISRO's plan to establish its second launch centre has been included in the Twelfth Five-Year Plan. A committee has also been formed and is studying the selection of a site for the new launch centre.

Southern Tamil Nadu has not achieved the expected level of industrial progress. All the basic geographical conditions necessary for industrial development exist there, and the region also has abundant skilled human resources. It is therefore essential that the Union government give the region due attention and select it for industrial-development projects.

Establishing an “Indian Space and Liquid-Propulsion Centre” at Mahendragiri in Tirunelveli district and a “second rocket launch centre” at Kulasekarapattinam in Thoothukudi district would certainly transform that entire region into a developed zone. It would also make the region a seedbed for a technological revolution in our country.
I therefore asked the Prime Minister in that letter to take immediate and appropriate action to establish these two important institutions.

It was in this situation that the Mangalyaan spacecraft was successfully launched last week from the Satish Dhawan Space Centre at Sriharikota, on India's first mission to study Mars. Built by Indian scientists at a cost of 450 crore rupees, the spacecraft has drawn the world's attention to our scientists by being placed with technical precision on its planned course towards Mars. Travelling about 440 million kilometres from Earth over roughly 300 days, it is due to reach the vicinity of Mars in September 2014 and conduct its studies there. India has become the fourth participant in this effort, after the United States, Russia and the European Union. This is news in which every Indian can take pride.

I have already conveyed my heartfelt praise and congratulations to all the scientists who performed this splendid work under the leadership of ISRO Chairman K. Radhakrishnan. Yet there is a lingering doubt that ISRO, which has brought India pride and joy, continues to treat Tamil Nadu with bitterness and indifference.

Under the Twelfth Five-Year Plan, sixty projects a year were envisaged to promote space research, and substantial funds were allocated for them. The Satish Dhawan Space Centre at Sriharikota in Andhra Pradesh already has two rocket launch pads. Because many satellite projects are under way and several countries approach India to launch their satellites, ISRO decided to establish another launch pad there itself. But many experts associated with ISRO insisted that Kulasekarapattinam in Thoothukudi district was the most suitable site. They say that ISRO did not even take this proposal up for consideration and has already begun work on a third launch pad at Sriharikota.

Officials themselves regret that some people have diverted this project, worth about 12,000 crore rupees, even though a launch pad at Kulasekarapattinam is the best choice scientifically, geographically and from the standpoint of security, and could save many crores of rupees from the usual costs.
M. Manoharan, General Secretary of the Liquid Propulsion Centre Employees' Union, explained the matter with these figures:

> Kulasekarapattinam is a far better site for a rocket launch pad than Sriharikota. This can be established through the technical details alone. PSLV satellites must be launched southward and placed at distances ranging from 450 to 1,000 kilometres. GSLV rockets must be launched eastward over the equator and placed at a distance of 36,000 kilometres. > > Under international space rules, rockets launched by one country must not fly over another country. But if rockets are launched directly from Sriharikota, they may fly over Sri Lanka and Indonesia. They must therefore first be sent south-east, redirected, and then brought into orbit. This adds many crores of rupees to the cost. > > The fuel section of a PSLV rocket has four stages. The fourth stage is attached solely to carry the additional fuel needed to go around Sri Lanka. That stage alone costs about twenty crore rupees. Launching the rocket from Kulasekarapattinam would avoid this. > > A satellite's usefulness is determined by its weight. A heavier satellite can carry more transponders and research instruments. From Sriharikota, a PSLV rocket can place a 1,600-kilogram satellite at a distance of 650 kilometres. From Kulasekarapattinam, however, it would be possible to launch a 2,200-kilogram satellite, giving an additional payload of 600 kilograms. In today's international market, sending one kilogram into space costs between twelve and eighteen lakh rupees. On that basis, the cost of the additional 600 kilograms is 90 crore rupees. A launch from Kulasekarapattinam would therefore provide both additional payload and savings. > > If satellites are launched from Kulasekarapattinam on GSLV rockets, they can cover the distance of 36,000 kilometres in less travel time. Once the target is reached, the satellite's life is determined by the fuel remaining. The shorter journey saves fuel and can extend the satellite's life by as much as two years. Launching one PSLV rocket from Kulasekarapattinam would therefore save at least 110 crore rupees. > > A total of 12,000 crore rupees has been allocated for the third launch pad. That entire amount could be recovered through the savings from 109 PSLV launches. There are many problems with establishing additional launch pads in a single location. > > Because Sriharikota is highly vulnerable to cyclones, work comes to a halt for two months each year, reducing productivity. If an unexpected accident occurs at one launch pad, the other rocket facilities too will be affected. A natural calamity or an attack by foreign forces could destroy the entire technological establishment.

Several reasons are given for the neglect of Kulasekarapattinam. Kerala and Andhra Pradesh are said to wield the greatest influence within ISRO. Some people seem to operate with the stubborn attitude that even if their own state does not receive something, Tamil Nadu must not get it. Sixty per cent of rocket-production work takes place at Mahendragiri in Tamil Nadu, but recruitment for that work is conducted in Thiruvananthapuram. As a result, people from only certain states are recruited.

As soon as the decision was taken to establish a new rocket launch pad, 300 acres of land were acquired in haste in Krishna district, Andhra Pradesh. After trade unions protested, a committee headed by Professor Narayana was formed and instructed to select a site and submit its report by February 2013. Four members of that committee were from Andhra Pradesh, two from Kerala, and only one was a Tamil. The committee did not inspect Kulasekarapattinam at all. ISRO officials who met the Chief Minister of Andhra Pradesh regarding a site did not even meet the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu, they say.

But Tamil Nadu needs a government that regards the people's welfare as paramount, thinks about such rare opportunities for industrial development, and acts with energy. Is anyone in Tamil Nadu thinking about any of this now?

I wrote to the Prime Minister about the Kulasekarapattinam launch centre as early as last August, and the news appeared in the newspapers. What action did the Tamil Nadu government take after that? Whatever we may write to the Prime Minister, the request will gain importance only if the Tamil Nadu government places the demand on the state's behalf. Why has the Tamil Nadu government not come forward?
If the rocket launch centre is established at Kulasekarapattinam, it will directly employ 4,000 people and indirectly employ 10,000.
Investments of up to three lakh crore rupees could follow. Through them, 30,000 to 50,000 people could gain direct employment and about one lakh people indirect employment.
The establishment of an educational institution for liquid propulsion would create opportunities for large numbers of scientists to emerge from Tamil Nadu.
Rail facilities would improve. Roads would be widened. Thoothukudi airport would be converted into an international airport.

For all these benefits to be realised, the launch centre must be established at Kulasekarapattinam. And for that launch centre to be established, Tamil Nadu must have a government determined to make it happen. That alone will be good for the country! Good for the people of the country too!
With affection,
M.K.
